127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)

129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**

131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. 1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean
5	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/Vestigial.lean
6	
7	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
8	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
9	
10	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
11	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
12	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
13	\[
14	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
15	  \qquad
16	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
17	\]
18	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
19	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
20	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
21	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
22	
23	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
24	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
25	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
26	% groups)
27	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
28	% SOURCE QUOTE:
29	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
30	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
31	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
32	% abelian groups defined by the formula
33	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
34	% \end{dfn}
35	%  [\dots]
36	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
37	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
39	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
40	% topologies  by
41	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
42	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
43	%  \end{dfn}"
44	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
45	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
46	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
47	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
48	homomorphisms.}
49	
50	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
51	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
52	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
53	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
54	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
55	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
56	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
57	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
58	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
59	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
60	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
61	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
62	structure. This is the content of
63	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
64	
65	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
66	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
67	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
68	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
69	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
70	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
71	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
72	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
73	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
74	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
75	\begin{enumerate}
76	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
77	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
78	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
79	    isomorphism classes;
80	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
81	    for \(\otimes\);
82	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
83	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
84	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
85	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
86	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
87	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
88	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
89	\end{enumerate}
90	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
91	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
92	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
93	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
94	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
95	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
96	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
97	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
98	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
99	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
100	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
101	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
102	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
103	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
104	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
105	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
106	supplied.
107	
108	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
109	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
110	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
111	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
112	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
113	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
114	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
115	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
116	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
117	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
118	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
119	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
120	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
121	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
122	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
123	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
124	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
125	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
126	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
127	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
128	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
129	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
130	avoids. The
131	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
132	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
133	The \emph{full} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent
134	monoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical
135	path; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the
136	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a
137	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
138	stalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly
139	\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.
140

146	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
147	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than

267	commutation; so the stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) itself is on the critical path, even
268	though the full coherent monoidal category built from it is not.

278	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side

281	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on

284	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. The full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent monoidal category that route~(e) would

290	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category, not the stalk infrastructure.

293	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
294	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)

296	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
297	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate

300	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
301	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
302	
303	\begin{definition}
304	
305	
306	\leanok
307	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
308	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
310	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
311	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
312	  \[
313	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
314	  \]
315	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
316	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
317	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
318	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
319	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
320	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
321	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
322	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
323	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
324	  canonical compatibility
325	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
326	     \;\cong\;
327	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
328	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
329	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
330	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
331	
332	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
333	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
334	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
335	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
336	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
337	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
338	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
339	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
340	\end{definition}
341	
342	\begin{lemma}
343	
344	
345	\leanok
346	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
347	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
349	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
350	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
351	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
352	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
353	  \[
354	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
355	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
356	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
357	  \]
358	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
359	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
360	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
361	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
362	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
363	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
364	  \[
365	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
366	    \qquad
367	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
368	  \]
369	  the associator
370	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
371	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
372	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
373	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
374	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
375	\end{lemma}
376	
377	\begin{proof}
378	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
379	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
380	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
381	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
382	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
383	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
384	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
385	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
386	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
387	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
388	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
389	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
390	\end{proof}
391	
392	\begin{remark}
393	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
394	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
395	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
396	        lem:whisker_of_W}
397	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
398	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
399	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
400	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
401	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
402	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
403	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
404	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
405	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
406	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
407	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
408	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
409	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
410	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
411	
412	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
413	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
414	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
415	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
416	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
417	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
418	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
419	  fixed-base presheaves in
420	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
421	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
422	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
423	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). The \emph{full}
424	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal
425	  category are not on the critical path: the group law is realized without them. The
426	  single left-whisker existence lemma and its underlying d.1/d.2 stalk apparatus, by
427	  contrast, \emph{are} on the critical path, since the associator's sheafification
428	  transport consumes exactly that one whisker obligation
429	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via d.2).
430	
431	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
432	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
433	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
434	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
435	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
436	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
437	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
438	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
439	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
440	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
441	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
442	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
443	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
444	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
445	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
446	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
447	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
448	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
449	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
450	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
451	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
452	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
453	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
454	\end{remark}

456	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
457	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
458	
459	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
460	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
461	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
462	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
463	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the

2564	\section{Pullback-monoidality: invertibility under a general scheme morphism}
2565	\label{sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality}
2566	
2567	The relative Picard consumer (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) re-bases its
2568	line-bundle carrier onto the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2569	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and its
2570	functorial structure maps are \emph{pullback} maps of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the
2571	projection pullback \(\pi_T^*\) along \(C \times_S T \to T\) and the base-change
2572	pullback along \(C \times_S T' \to C \times_S T\). For these maps to descend to the
2573	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) carrier one needs that \emph{pullback preserves
2574	\(\otimes\)-invertibility} for \emph{general} morphisms of schemes --- the
2575	projection and the base-change morphism are neither open immersions nor flat.
2576	
2577	This is a genuinely different fact from the open-immersion compatibility
2578	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and not a corollary of it. That lemma handles
2579	restriction along an open immersion, and it does so by routing through the
2580	\emph{lax right} adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is
2581	strong monoidal there \emph{only} because an open immersion's structure-sheaf ring
2582	map is a local \emph{isomorphism} (\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\)). A general scheme
2583	morphism has no such isomorphism, so the right-adjoint route is unavailable.
2584	
2585	The general case is the \emph{left} adjoint, pullback \(f^* = \) inverse image
2586	\(=(\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\). Conceptually pullback is
2587	``extension of scalars'', and over a single ring extension of scalars is indeed
2588	strong monoidal. But the sheaf-of-modules pullback is defined \emph{abstractly} as
2589	a left adjoint: it has \emph{no} sectionwise value formula and \emph{no} stalkwise
2590	value formula, so one cannot literally attach a sectionwise \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)
2591	tensorator to \((f^*).\mathrm{obj}\). Moreover the doctrinal transfer that Mathlib
2592	ships, \(\mathtt{Adjunction.rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\), produces a \emph{lax}
2593	structure on the \emph{right} adjoint from an oplax structure on the left; the
2594	oplax comparison maps on the left adjoint itself are \emph{not} provided for free.
2595	Consequently the monoidality of \(f^*\) is established here by \emph{local-chart
2596	finality}, not by a global sectionwise tensorator: one exhibits the comparison maps
2597	\(f^*(M \otimes N) \to f^*M \otimes f^*N\) and \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_Y\)
2598	and proves each is an isomorphism by restricting to affine charts on which the
2599	comparison functor is \(\mathtt{Final}\) --- precisely the charts where the pullback
2600	\emph{is} locally extension of scalars --- and then globalizing ``iso on every chart
2601	\(\Rightarrow\) global iso''. This is the whole reason the general lemma is a separate
2602	result, with its own \emph{two}-phase proof (the cheap unit comparison and the
2603	genuinely new tensor comparison).
2604	
2605	It is worth noting why the monoidality route is the primary one rather than a
2606	detour. Restricting to \emph{flat} structure maps does \emph{not} help: flatness
2607	governs the exactness of \(f^*\), not whether the tensor comparison
2608	\(f^*(M \otimes N) \to f^*M \otimes f^*N\) is an isomorphism (that comparison is an
2609	isomorphism for \emph{every} morphism, flat or not), so a flat restriction would
2610	simplify nothing here. The only genuinely simpler-looking alternative --- routing
2611	through local triviality, ``invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally \(\mathcal{O}\)
2612	\(\Rightarrow\) pulls back to locally \(\mathcal{O}\)'' --- is a mirage on this
2613	carrier: recovering \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) from local triviality is the reverse
2614	bridge \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsInvertible}\), i.e.\ exactly
2615	the dual/tensor-inverse manufacture that the invertibility-carrier pivot
2616	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}) was built to delete. The pullback-monoidality
2617	route is therefore primary precisely because it pulls back the existing tensor-inverse
2618	witness directly, never reintroducing the deleted dual.

# Analogy: strong-monoidal tensorator for an abstract left-adjoint pullback of sheaves of modules

## Mode
cross-domain-inspiration

## Slug
pullback-tensor

## Iteration
242

## Structural problem (abstracted)
Given a monoidal adjunction-shaped situation `F ⊣ G` between two monoidal categories
(here `F = pullback`, `G = pushforward`, objects = sheaves of modules over a varying
structure ring, `⊗ = sheafify(presheaf-⊗)`), with `F` defined ONLY as the abstract
left adjoint `(pushforward).leftAdjoint` (no sectionwise/stalkwise value), produce a
strong-monoidal comparison `F(A ⊗ B) ≅ F A ⊗ F B` — at least enough to conclude
`IsInvertible M → IsInvertible (F M)`.

## Failed approaches (from directive)
- Sectionwise `extendScalars.Monoidal`: the abstract left adjoint has no sectionwise
  formula to apply it to.
- Generic `Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal` packager: *directive claimed absent* —
  **this is STALE (see below): it is present at the pin.**
- Filesystem grep: no tensor-pullback comparison, no `MonoidalCategory (SheafOfModules)`.

## KEY CORRECTION TO THE DIRECTIVE'S PREMISE
The pinned Mathlib (`b80f227`, 2026-04-20) **does** contain the full doctrinal-adjunction
tensorator machinery. PR #36599 ("the monoidal adjunction between the extension and the
restriction of scalars", merged 2026-03-20) added it; the pin postdates it. So Q1's
"has Mathlib built a tensorator from an adjoint?" = **YES**, and Q3's bump question is
moot — it is already in the tree.

Locations (all in `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Functor.lean`):
- `Adjunction.rightAdjointLaxMonoidal` (line 897): `F ⊣ G`, `[F.OplaxMonoidal] ⊢ G.LaxMonoidal`.
- `Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal` (line 1009): `F ⊣ G`, `[G.LaxMonoidal] ⊢ F.OplaxMonoidal`.
  Explicit mate formula (line 1010-1011):
    `η := (adj.homEquiv _ _).symm (ε G)`
    `δ X Y := (adj.homEquiv _ _).symm ((adj.unit.app X ⊗ₘ adj.unit.app Y) ≫ μ G _ _)`
- `Adjunction.IsMonoidal` (line 952): compatibility Prop; auto-instances `instIsMonoidal`,
  `instIsMonoidal_1` produce it for the mate structures.
- `Adjunction.laxMonoidalEquivOplaxMonoidal` (line 1070): `G.LaxMonoidal ≃ F.OplaxMonoidal`.

## Analogues found

### Analogue: `Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal` (doctrinal adjunction / mate)
- **Domain**: category theory — monoidal functors / mates.
- **Same structural problem there**: produce a (op)lax tensorator on a functor given only
  as one half of an adjunction, transporting structure across the adjunction by the mate
  correspondence (no value formula for the functor needed).
- **Technique**: the tensorator `δ_{X,Y} : F(X⊗Y) → FX⊗FY` is the *adjunct* of
  `(unit_X ⊗ unit_Y) ≫ μ_G : X⊗Y → G(FX) ⊗ G(FY) → G(FX⊗FY)`, i.e.
  `δ X Y := (adj.homEquiv _ _).symm ((adj.unit.app X ⊗ₘ adj.unit.app Y) ≫ μ G _ _)`.
  This needs ONLY the lax structure `μ_G` on the right adjoint + the adjunction unit; it is
  exactly "tensorator without a sectionwise value."
- **Mapping to project**: `F = Scheme.Modules.pullback f`, `G = Scheme.Modules.pushforward f`,
  `adj = Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f` (this term EXISTS in Mathlib,
  `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Modules/Sheaf.lean:172`). `μ_G` = the lax comparison
  `f_*A ⊗ f_*B → f_*(A⊗B)` for pushforward.
- **Porting cost**: HIGH for the bundled API, MEDIUM for a hand-port. Blockers:
  1. The bundled API requires `MonoidalCategory (SheafOfModules 𝒪_X)` and
     `MonoidalCategory (SheafOfModules 𝒪_Y)` instances — **the project has none** (it
     carries `Scheme.Modules.tensorObj` as a bespoke sheafified-presheaf tensor with no
     bundled monoidal category, which is *why* the group law was built by hand on
     `IsInvertible`). So `[F.OplaxMonoidal]`/`[G.LaxMonoidal]` cannot even be stated as-is.
  2. Need `pushforward.LaxMonoidal` (the `μ_G` above) — **absent from Mathlib** (grep of
     `SheafOfModules.pushforward*` shows no monoidal structure).
  3. **Oplax gives only a MAP, not an iso.** `pullbackTensorIso` must be an iso; oplax `δ`
     is not automatically invertible. Strong monoidality (`F.Monoidal`, each `δ` iso) is
     genuine geometric content the adjunction alone does NOT supply.
  The hand-port: skip the `MonoidalCategory` instance, build the single comparison map
  `δ_{M,N}` for the specific `M,N` via the formula above (needs `μ` only for `f_*(f^*M), f_*(f^*N)`),
  then prove it is iso separately.
- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND (precedent confirmed; the directive's "absent" was wrong),
  but NOT plug-and-play — see Analogue 2 for the route Mathlib itself actually uses.

### Analogue: `ModuleCat.extendScalars.Monoidal` + `Monoidal/Adjunction.lean` (the sectionwise model — and Mathlib's OWN recipe)
- **Domain**: commutative algebra / ModuleCat — base change of modules.
- **Same structural problem there**: extension of scalars `extendScalars f : Mod R → Mod S`
  along `f : R →+* S` (the LEFT adjoint of `restrictScalars`) is the affine, sectionwise
  model of pullback; it must be strong monoidal.
- **Technique — and the decisive observation**: Mathlib does **NOT** get the LEFT adjoint's
  monoidal structure from the adjunction. In `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean`:
  - `instance : (extendScalars f).Monoidal` (line 42) is built **explicitly** via
    `Functor.CoreMonoidal.toMonoidal`, with the tensorator coming from the linear-algebra
    distributivity iso `TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.distribBaseChange`
    (`S ⊗_R (M ⊗_R N) ≅ (S⊗_R M) ⊗_S (S⊗_R N)`). This is a genuine *iso*, hence STRONG.
  - `instance : (restrictScalars f).LaxMonoidal := (extendRestrictScalarsAdj f).rightAdjointLaxMonoidal`
    (line 102) — the RIGHT adjoint's lax structure is THEN derived from the adjunction.
  So the Mathlib-endorsed pattern is: **build the left adjoint's strong-monoidal structure
  concretely (CoreMonoidal + a distributivity iso), and derive the right adjoint's lax
  structure from the adjunction — not the other way round.**
- **Mapping to project**: build a *concrete* strong-monoidal pullback
  `P := sheafify ∘ (presheaf base-change)` (presheaf base change = sectionwise `extendScalars`,
  whose strong monoidal structure is the lemma above; sheafification is monoidal — the project
  already has the brick `sheafifyTensorUnitIso`), show `P ⊣ pushforward`, then transport the
  monoidal structure to the abstract `pullback` via uniqueness of left adjoints
  (`Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq` — the project already used this in iter-217 to close
  `tensorObj_restrict_iso`). The directive's "no sectionwise formula" obstacle is dissolved by
  `leftAdjointUniq`: the abstract adjoint is iso to any concrete one, and the monoidal
  structure transports along that iso.
- **Porting cost**: HIGH but this is the *honest* cost of strong-monoidality, which no route
  avoids. Ingredients mostly present: `distribBaseChange` ✓, sheafification-monoidal brick ✓,
  `leftAdjointUniq` ✓, `pullbackPushforwardAdjunction` ✓. Missing: the concrete presheaf
  base-change pullback as a monoidal functor + the monoidal transport along `leftAdjointUniq`.
- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND — the route the project should actually follow.

### Analogue: `Module.Invertible` / `CommRing.Pic` localization-stability (Q2 locally-free route)
- **Domain**: commutative algebra — Picard group of a ring.
- **Same structural problem there**: invertibility of a module is stable under base change /
  localization: `Module.Invertible.instLocalizationLocalizedModule`
  (`Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean`) — `[Module.Invertible R M] ⊢
  Module.Invertible (Localization S) (LocalizedModule S M)`. Plus
  `Module.Invertible.free_iff_linearEquiv`, `mk_eq_one_iff_free` characterize invertible =
  locally-free-rank-1.
- **Technique**: stalk-/localization-local reasoning: invertibility checked after base change
  to local rings, where invertible ⇔ free of rank 1.
- **Mapping to project**: the project ALREADY has the "pullback preserves local triviality"
  half — `IsLocallyTrivial.pullback` (`LineBundlePullback.lean:156`, DONE). So the Q2 route
  reduces to the bridge `IsLocallyTrivial M → IsInvertible M` (build the tensor-inverse of a
  locally-trivial sheaf), which is the project's KNOWN-STUCK arc
  (`dual_restrict_iso`/`exists_tensorObj_inverse`, demoted per [[ts232-carrier-pivot]]).
- **Porting cost**: HIGH and OFF the project's chosen path. Mathlib's results are over a single
  ring, not sheaves; there is NO Mathlib "stalk of the *abstract* pullback ≅ tensor of stalks"
  (only `Scheme.Modules.restrictStalkNatIso` for OPEN IMMERSIONS, not general `f`). The
  locally-free⇒tensor-invertible gluing remains the gap.
- **Verdict**: PARTIAL_ANALOGUE — higher cost/risk than the monoidal route; do not prefer.

## Important negative result (kills a tempting shortcut)
"Oplax/lax monoidal functors preserve invertible objects automatically" is **FALSE**. Global
sections `Γ` is lax monoidal yet sends line bundles to modules that need not be invertible
(e.g. `Γ(P¹, O(1)) = 0`). So even after obtaining the oplax `δ` from `leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal`,
one cannot skip proving `δ` is an iso. Strong monoidality on the relevant objects is genuinely
required; there is no free preservation lemma to lean on.

## Top suggestion
Mirror Mathlib's own recipe from PR #36599 (Analogue 2). Read
`Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean` (especially the
`(extendScalars f).Monoidal` instance at line 42 and `restrictScalars.LaxMonoidal` at line 102)
together with `Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal` in
`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Functor.lean:1009`. Then in the project: build a *concrete*
strong-monoidal presheaf-level pullback (sectionwise `extendScalars` via
`AlgebraTensorModule.distribBaseChange`, sheafified using the existing `sheafifyTensorUnitIso`
brick), prove it left-adjoint to `pushforward`, and transport its `Monoidal` structure to the
abstract `Scheme.Modules.pullback` along `Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq` (already used in iter-217).
That yields `pullbackTensorIso` as a genuine iso. Do NOT attempt to derive the left adjoint's
structure from a (still-unbuilt) lax pushforward — that is the wrong direction and Mathlib avoids
it. First project file to touch: a new monoidal-pullback file feeding
`AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean`.

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp,
        lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}
  This is the cheap half, reachable directly. The canonical comparison map is the
  genuine Mathlib morphism
  \[
    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\,f :
      f^*\mathcal{O}_X \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
  \]
  the unit comparison \(\eta\) of \(f^*\); abbreviate it \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\). Mathlib
  already proves this map is an isomorphism whenever the comparison functor is
  \(\mathtt{Final}\): the instance
  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsIsoPullbackObjUnitToUnitOfFinal}\). This instance is
  used in the proof of the open-immersion case
  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}\): for an open immersion the comparison functor
  is \(\mathtt{Final}\) outright, so \(\mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\) is an
  isomorphism on the nose.

  For a \emph{general} morphism \(f\) the comparison functor need not be
  \(\mathtt{Final}\) globally, so the isomorphism is established by reduction to
  local charts. Cover \(Y\) by affine charts; on such a chart \(V \subseteq Y\)
  factor the inclusion \(V.\iota \mathbin{;} f = g \mathbin{;} U.\iota\) through
  \(g = f.\mathtt{resLE}\,U\,V\), whose comparison functor
  \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,g.\mathtt{base}\) \emph{is} \(\mathtt{Final}\) (via
  \(\mathtt{final\_of\_representablyFlat}\)).

  The composition coherence that ties the comparison of a composite to the comparisons
  of its factors is now established: it is the pullback-side identity
  \[
    \mathrm{pbu}(V.\iota \mathbin{;} f)
      \;=\;
    (\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,V.\iota\,f).\mathrm{inv} \;\mathbin{;}\;
      (\mathtt{pullback}\,V.\iota).\mathrm{map}\bigl(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\bigr) \;\mathbin{;}\;
      \mathrm{pbu}\,(V.\iota)
  \]
  of \cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}, obtained by adjunction-mate transport from
  the pushforward-side coherence \cref{lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp}. What is
  reused from the open-immersion case is therefore only: the per-chart factorization
  \(V.\iota \mathbin{;} f = g \mathbin{;} U.\iota\) with \(g = f.\mathtt{resLE}\,U\,V\),
  the \(\mathtt{Final}\)-chart instance
  \(\mathtt{instIsIsoPullbackObjUnitToUnitOfFinal}\) (which makes
  \(\mathrm{pbu}\) for an open immersion or a \(\mathtt{Final}\)-chart morphism an
  isomorphism), and the project globalizer \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_restrict}\)
  (the same one used in \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).

  The sole remaining step is to assemble these into the global isomorphism. On each
  chart the coherence equation exhibits \(\mathrm{pbu}(V.\iota \mathbin{;} f)\) as a
  composite of three isomorphisms --- the pseudofunctoriality iso
  \((\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,V.\iota\,f).\mathrm{inv}\), the image under
  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,V.\iota\) of the \(\mathtt{Final}\)-chart isomorphism
  \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\) (here \(f\) is the \(\mathtt{Final}\)-chart morphism \(g\) after
  the factorization), and the open-immersion isomorphism \(\mathrm{pbu}\,(V.\iota)\) ---
  hence \(\mathrm{pbu}(V.\iota \mathbin{;} f)\) is itself an isomorphism on every chart.
  Transporting this per-chart isomorphism across
  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) identifies it with the restriction of the
  global comparison, and the globalizer \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_restrict}\) upgrades
  ``iso on every chart'' to the global statement. The result is the asserted
  isomorphism \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\).
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  This is a one-step consequence of a Mathlib instance, with no chart-chase. The
  canonical comparison map is the genuine Mathlib morphism
  \[
    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\,f :
      f^*\mathcal{O}_X \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
  \]
  the unit comparison \(\eta\) of \(f^*\); abbreviate it \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\). Mathlib
  proves this map is an isomorphism whenever the comparison functor
  \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}\) on opens is \(\mathtt{Final}\): the instance
  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsIsoPullbackObjUnitToUnitOfFinal}\).

  The point is that the finality hypothesis holds \emph{unconditionally}, for every
  morphism of schemes \(f\). The preimage functor \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}
  : \mathtt{Opens}\,X \to \mathtt{Opens}\,Y\) is a frame homomorphism: it preserves
  arbitrary unions and finite intersections, hence preserves all finite limits. A
  functor between small categories that preserves finite limits is representably flat,
  so \(\mathtt{final\_of\_representablyFlat}\) gives \((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}).\mathtt{Final}\)
  with no hypothesis on \(f\) whatsoever. (There is no need to pass to affine charts:
  finality is not a special feature of open immersions but a general property of
  preimage-on-opens.)

  Consequently the instance \(\mathtt{instIsIsoPullbackObjUnitToUnitOfFinal}\) fires
  directly, and \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\) is an isomorphism for every \(f\). The asserted
  isomorphism \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\) is the bundled iso
  \(\mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\,f\) of this comparison.
\end{proof}

The general case is the \emph{left} adjoint, pullback \(f^* = \) inverse image
\(=(\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\). Conceptually pullback is
``extension of scalars'', and over a single ring extension of scalars is indeed
strong monoidal. But the sheaf-of-modules pullback is defined \emph{abstractly} as
a left adjoint: it has \emph{no} sectionwise value formula and \emph{no} stalkwise
value formula, so one cannot literally attach a sectionwise \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)
tensorator to \((f^*).\mathrm{obj}\). Moreover the doctrinal transfer that Mathlib
ships, \(\mathtt{Adjunction.rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\), produces a \emph{lax}
structure on the \emph{right} adjoint from an oplax structure on the left; the
oplax comparison maps on the left adjoint itself are \emph{not} provided for free.
Consequently the monoidality of \(f^*\) is established here by \emph{local-chart
finality}, not by a global sectionwise tensorator: one exhibits the comparison maps
\(f^*(M \otimes N) \to f^*M \otimes f^*N\) and \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_Y\)
and proves each is an isomorphism by restricting to affine charts on which the
comparison functor is \(\mathtt{Final}\) --- precisely the charts where the pullback
\emph{is} locally extension of scalars --- and then globalizing ``iso on every chart
\(\Rightarrow\) global iso''. This is the whole reason the general lemma is a separate
result, with its own \emph{two}-phase proof (the cheap unit comparison and the
genuinely new tensor comparison).

It is worth noting why the monoidality route is the primary one rather than a
detour. Restricting to \emph{flat} structure maps does \emph{not} help: flatness
governs the exactness of \(f^*\), not whether the tensor comparison
\(f^*(M \otimes N) \to f^*M \otimes f^*N\) is an isomorphism (that comparison is an
isomorphism for \emph{every} morphism, flat or not), so a flat restriction would
simplify nothing here. The only genuinely simpler-looking alternative --- routing
through local triviality, ``invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally \(\mathcal{O}\)
\(\Rightarrow\) pulls back to locally \(\mathcal{O}\)'' --- is a mirage on this
carrier: recovering \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) from local triviality is the reverse
bridge \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsInvertible}\), i.e.\ exactly
the dual/tensor-inverse manufacture that the invertibility-carrier pivot
(\cref{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}) was built to delete. The pullback-monoidality
route is therefore primary precisely because it pulls back the existing tensor-inverse
witness directly, never reintroducing the deleted dual.

The general case is the \emph{left} adjoint, pullback \(f^* = \) inverse image
\(=(\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\). Conceptually pullback \emph{is}
``extension of scalars'', and extension of scalars along any ring map \emph{is} strong
monoidal --- the base-change tensorator \((M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S \cong
(M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S (N \otimes_R S)\) is an isomorphism with no flatness
hypothesis. So at the level of mathematics there is no obstacle: pullback commutes
with \(\otimes\) for every morphism. The one genuine subtlety is purely structural to
the formalisation: the project carries \(f^*\) \emph{abstractly}, as the left adjoint
\((\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\), so that \((f^*).\mathrm{obj}\) has
no sectionwise value to which one could literally attach the \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)
tensorator. The resolution is the standard one for an abstract left adjoint: build the
strong-monoidal structure on a \emph{concrete} model \(P\) of the pullback and transport
it to \(f^*\) along the uniqueness of left adjoints. This dissolves the
``no sectionwise formula'' worry entirely --- one never computes \(f^*\) sectionwise;
one transports a concrete monoidal structure across an adjoint-uniqueness isomorphism.
This is exactly the device that closed \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.

It is worth recording why two tempting shortcuts are not taken. First, one might hope
that since pullback is \emph{some} (op)lax monoidal functor, invertible objects are
preserved for free. This is \emph{false}: global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal,
yet \(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not an invertible module, so a mere
oplax comparison map does \emph{not} suffice --- one genuinely has to prove the tensor
comparison is an \emph{isomorphism}. Second, one might route through local triviality,
``invertible \(\Rightarrow\) locally \(\mathcal{O}\) \(\Rightarrow\) pulls back to
locally \(\mathcal{O}\)''. This is a mirage on the present carrier: recovering
\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) from local triviality is the reverse bridge
\(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsInvertible}\), i.e.\ exactly the
dual/tensor-inverse manufacture that the invertibility-carrier pivot
(\cref{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}) was built to delete. Restricting to \emph{flat}
structure maps does not help either: flatness governs the exactness of \(f^*\), not
whether the tensor comparison is an isomorphism (it is an isomorphism for \emph{every}
morphism, flat or not). The strong-monoidal pullback route is therefore primary
precisely because it pulls back the existing tensor-inverse witness directly, through a
comparison isomorphism valid for all morphisms, never reintroducing the deleted dual.

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:pullback_unit_iso}
  Because the abstract left-adjoint pullback has no sectionwise value, the
  comparison map must first be \emph{constructed} and then shown to be an isomorphism
  by reduction to local charts. The argument has three parts: (a) build the
  comparison map, (b) prove it is an isomorphism by a finality chart-chase, (c) observe
  that this pointwise isomorphism is all the invertibility consumer needs (the full
  strong-monoidal packaging is off the critical path).

  \emph{(a) The tensor comparison map.} In parallel to the canonical unit comparison
  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\) of \cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso},
  one constructs a tensor comparison
  \[
    \mathtt{pullbackObjTensorToTensor} :
      f^*(M \otimes_X N)
      \;\longrightarrow\;
      (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N),
  \]
  the oplax monoidal comparison \(\delta\) of \(f^*\) on the pair \((M, N)\). This map
  is not available off the shelf --- the Mathlib pullback ships only the composition
  coherences \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\), \(\mathtt{pullbackId}\),
  \(\mathtt{pullback\_assoc}\), and \emph{no} tensorator. It is produced from the
  monoidal structure of the \emph{presheaf}-level pullback
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) (where, unlike the sheaf level, the
  ambient \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}\) on presheaves of modules over a
  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued ring presheaf \emph{does} exist), moved across the
  sheafification using the genuine Mathlib lemma
  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\) --- general (any morphism of
  ring-sheaves) and already consumed in \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- which
  commutes \(f^*\) with sheafification. The reconciliation of the right-hand side
  after this move is supplied by the already-established sheafification-monoidality
  brick (``sheafification is monoidal up to the unit'', cf.
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), which the eventual comparison consumes.

  \emph{(b) It is an isomorphism, by local-chart finality.} This is the same
  chart-chase that proves the unit comparison in \cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}, run one
  level up. Cover \(Y\) by affine charts; on such a chart \(V \subseteq Y\) factor the
  inclusion as \(V.\iota \mathbin{;} f = g \mathbin{;} U.\iota\) through
  \(g = f.\mathtt{resLE}\,U\,V\), whose comparison functor
  \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,g.\mathtt{base}\) is \(\mathtt{Final}\) (via
  \(\mathtt{final\_of\_representablyFlat}\)). On a \(\mathtt{Final}\) chart the
  pullback \emph{is} locally extension of scalars, which is \emph{strong monoidal} in
  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\): the local comparison is exactly the \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)
  tensorator \((M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S \cong (M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S
  (N \otimes_R S)\), an isomorphism for every ring map with no hypothesis. The
  restricted global \(\mathtt{pullbackObjTensorToTensor}\) is identified with this
  local comparison through the naturality of the
  \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\)/\(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\)/\(\mathtt{pullbackCongr}\)
  cluster --- the same glue wired in \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}\). Finally
  ``iso on every chart \(\Rightarrow\) global iso'' is the project's
  \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_restrict}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}'s
  globalizer). The residual work is the naturality lemma tying the restricted global
  comparison to the local one.

  \emph{(c) The pointwise isomorphism is all that is needed.} The sole consumer of
  this lemma on the critical path, \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback}, requires only the
  comparison \emph{isomorphism at the single witness pair} \((M, N)\) together with its
  naturality --- \emph{not} a strong-monoidal functor structure on \(f^*\). Parts
  (a)--(b) already deliver exactly that: the comparison
  \(\mathtt{pullbackObjTensorToTensor}\) at \((M, N)\), shown to be an isomorphism by the
  finality chart-chase. We therefore take the asserted isomorphism
  \(f^*(M \otimes_X N) \cong (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)\) to be \(\mathtt{asIso}\) of that
  pointwise comparison, with naturality inherited from the naturality of the comparison
  and of sheafification.

  Packaging the whole family \(\{\delta_{M,N}\}\) into a strong-monoidal structure on
  \(f^*\) via the Mathlib wrapper \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreMonoidal.ofOplaxMonoidal}\) (from
  \([\mathtt{IsIso}\,\eta]\) and \([\forall X Y,\ \mathtt{IsIso}\,(\delta_{X,Y})]\)) is
  possible, but it additionally requires a \emph{hand-built} \(\mathtt{OplaxMonoidal}\,f^*\)
  instance --- there is no \(\mathtt{leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\) transfer (Mathlib ships
  only \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\), the opposite direction), so the oplax
  structure with all its coherence is not free. That packaging is \emph{not} needed for
  the invertibility consumer and is deferred as off the critical path; the pointwise
  isomorphism above is the deliverable.
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
  Mathematically, pullback is extension of scalars and extension of scalars is strong
  monoidal, so the comparison is an isomorphism for every \(f\). The only work is to
  carry this through the formalisation's \emph{abstract} pullback \(f^* =
  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\), which has no sectionwise value. We
  do not compute \(f^*\) sectionwise; instead we equip a \emph{concrete} model of the
  pullback with a strong-monoidal structure and transport it to \(f^*\) along the
  uniqueness of left adjoints. This mirrors Mathlib's own construction of the
  extension-of-scalars monoidal structure in
  \texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean}, where the
  \emph{left} adjoint's strong-monoidal structure is built explicitly (via the
  base-change distributivity isomorphism) and only afterwards is the right adjoint's
  lax structure derived from the adjunction --- never the reverse direction. The
  argument has three steps.

  \emph{Step 1 (a concrete strong-monoidal pullback \(P\)).} Build the concrete functor
  \[
    P \;=\; \mathtt{sheafification} \;\circ\; (\text{presheaf base change}),
  \]
  the presheaf-level inverse image (sectionwise extension of scalars along the
  structure-sheaf ring maps of \(f\)) followed by sheafification on the small Zariski
  site --- the same \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\) shape already used in
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. Equip \(P\) with a strong-monoidal structure. Its
  tensorator is the linear-algebra strong-monoidal core
  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.distribBaseChange}\),
  \[
    (M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S
      \;\cong\;
    (M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S (N \otimes_R S),
  \]
  an isomorphism for \emph{every} ring map \(R \to S\), with no flatness, sheafified
  pointwise; this is precisely Mathlib's \((\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\varphi).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
  lifted to sheaves. The unit comparison is the already-landed sheafification-monoidality
  brick \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\). Thus \(P\) is strong monoidal with each
  tensorator and the unit comparison an isomorphism, with no hypothesis on \(f\).

  \emph{Step 2 (\(P\) is left adjoint to pushforward).} Exhibit an adjunction
  \(P \dashv \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforward}\,f\). This is the universal property
  of sheafified extension of scalars: the presheaf base change is left adjoint to
  presheaf restriction of scalars, and sheafification is left adjoint to the inclusion,
  so the composite \(P\) is left adjoint to the pushforward.

  \emph{Step 3 (transport to the abstract pullback).} By definition
  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f = (\mathtt{pushforward}\,f).\mathtt{leftAdjoint}\)
  is \emph{a} left adjoint of the pushforward. By the uniqueness of left adjoints
  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) --- the same device that closed
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- there is a canonical natural isomorphism
  \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f \cong P\). The strong-monoidal structure of
  \(P\) travels along this isomorphism, so its tensorator becomes a genuine
  \emph{isomorphism}
  \[
    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N),
  \]
  natural in \(M\) and \(N\). This is the asserted comparison; the abstract pullback is
  never evaluated sectionwise --- the monoidal structure is transported across the
  adjoint-uniqueness isomorphism.

  Two shortcuts are deliberately avoided. The bundled doctrinal adjunction
  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\) is present at the pinned commit, but
  it is not usable here: it requires \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{SheafOfModules})\)
  instances (the project carries \(\otimes_X\) as a bespoke sheafified tensor with no
  such instance and no \(\mathtt{pushforward.LaxMonoidal}\)), and in any case it would
  yield only an \emph{oplax} comparison \emph{map}, not the required isomorphism. And a
  mere oplax map does not suffice: an (op)lax monoidal functor does \emph{not} preserve
  invertible objects for free --- global sections \(\Gamma\) is lax monoidal yet
  \(\Gamma(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(1)) = 0\) is not invertible --- so proving the
  comparison is an isomorphism, as Step~3 does, is genuinely necessary.

  The full strong-monoidal packaging of \(f^*\) is delivered by the transported
  structure \(P.\mathtt{Monoidal}\); the invertibility consumer
  \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} consumes only the comparison \emph{isomorphism} at
  its witness pair, which Step~3 supplies.
\end{proof}

tos-pullback

COMPLETE

blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex

### EDIT A — proof of `lem:pullback_unit_iso` (`pullbackUnitIso`)
- **Revised** the `\begin{proof}` body: replaced the obsolete affine chart-chase
  (premised on the now-retracted false claim that `(Opens.map f.base).Final` "need not
  hold globally for a general morphism") with the correct one-line argument:
  `Opens.map f.base` is a frame homomorphism ⇒ preserves finite limits ⇒ representably
  flat ⇒ `final_of_representablyFlat` gives `.Final` **unconditionally** for every `f`
  ⇒ the Mathlib instance `instIsIsoPullbackObjUnitToUnitOfFinal` fires directly, and
  `pullbackUnitIso f` is the bundled iso of the comparison. No chart-chase.
- **Deleted** the false sentence "For a general morphism `f` the comparison functor need
  not be `Final` globally…" and the whole chart factorization / globalizer narrative.
- **Fixed the proof `\uses{}`**: removed `lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp` and
  `lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp` (the one-line proof does not consume them); kept
  `def:scheme_modules_tensorobj`.
- The lemma **statement** block (and its `\lean{...}`/`\label`) is untouched. The two
  standalone composition-coherence blocks `lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp` /
  `lem:unitToPushforwardObjUnit_comp` are **retained unchanged** (correct standalone).

### EDIT B — §6 narrative + proof of `lem:pullback_tensor_iso` (`pullbackTensorIso`)
- **Revised** the narrative paragraph under `\label{sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality}`
  that carried the retracted premise: removed the false claims that the abstract left
  adjoint forces monoidality to be shown "by local-chart finality" and that no
  sectionwise tensorator route exists. New prose: pullback **is** extension of scalars
  and **is** strong monoidal (base-change tensorator is an iso for every ring map, no
  flatness); the only subtlety is that the project's `f^*` is an abstract left adjoint,
  so the strong-monoidal structure is built on a concrete model `P` and transported via
  `leftAdjointUniq`. Folded in the two NEGATIVE results from the analyst: (i) "(op)lax
  monoidal ⇒ preserves invertibles for free" is FALSE (`Γ(P¹,O(1))=0`), so the iso must
  be proved; (ii) the locally-trivial route is rejected (revives the deleted
  `dual`/`exists_tensorObj_inverse` arc). Kept the correct "flatness doesn't help" point.
- **Revised** the `\begin{proof}` of `lem:pullback_tensor_iso` to the new three-step
  route (mirroring Mathlib `ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean`, PR #36599 named in prose
  only, no fabricated `% SOURCE`):
  1. concrete strong-monoidal `P = sheafify ∘ (sectionwise extension of scalars)`,
     tensorator = `AlgebraTensorModule.distribBaseChange` sheafified, unit =
     `sheafifyTensorUnitIso`;
  2. `P ⊣ pushforward f`;
  3. transport to `(pushforward f).leftAdjoint = pullback f` via `leftAdjointUniq`,
     yielding the genuine **isomorphism** natural in `M,N`.
  Removed the old "(a) build map / (b) finality chart-chase / (c) pointwise is enough"
  sketch and the trailing `Functor.CoreMonoidal.ofOplaxMonoidal` paragraph; replaced the
  latter with a one-line note that the full packaging is delivered by `P.Monoidal`.
- **Updated `\uses{}`** on both the statement and proof of `lem:pullback_tensor_iso` to
  `{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}` (the latter for the
  `sheafificationCompPullback` / `leftAdjointUniq` machinery genuinely cited).
- The lemma **statement** of `lem:pullback_tensor_iso`, its `% SOURCE` /
  `% SOURCE QUOTE` block (Stacks `lemma-tensor-product-pullback`), and the
  `\textit{Source: …}` line are **kept verbatim**.
